title: BugBash
one_liner: A lightweight client-side tool for logging, tagging, and triaging JavaScript bugs found during manual testing sessions.
problem_statement: Frontend developers and QA testers need a fast, frictionless way to capture and organize bugs discovered during manual testing or bug bash sessions without setting up a full issue tracker. Bugs found in the moment often get lost or lack enough context to reproduce. BugBash gives teams a zero-setup scratchpad to record, categorize, and prioritize frontend errors before they hit production.
target_user: Frontend developers, indie hackers, and small engineering teams who run manual bug bash sessions and need a quick way to capture JavaScript errors and UI regressions without spinning up Jira or a backend.
features: Bug Logger: Quickly add a bug entry with a title, severity level (low/medium/high/critical), URL of the affected page, and freeform description — all saved instantly to localStorage.
Bug Board: A kanban-style or list view showing all logged bugs grouped by severity or status (open, in progress, resolved), with inline editing and one-click status updates.
Session Summary Export: Generate a plain-text or JSON summary of all bugs logged in the current session, copyable to clipboard for pasting into Slack, GitHub Issues, or a doc.
non_goals: No backend storage, syncing, or real-time collaboration between users
No automatic JavaScript error capture or SDK integration — all entries are manual
No user accounts, login, or team management features
No screenshot or video recording capabilities
technical_constraints: Client-side only — no backend, API calls, or server-side rendering beyond Next.js static export
All data persisted exclusively via localStorage with no external database
No authentication or user identity of any kind
Built as a single-page Next.js app using React and Tailwind CSS
Must function fully offline after first load
